name: Piqueras and Santa Inés Pass Loop
name_original: Mirador de Piqueras y Puerto de Santa Inés — circular por el Parque Natural de Sierra Cebollera
description: Tackle the difficult 70.8-mile Mirador de Piqueras y Puerto de Santa Inés circular road cycling route through Sierra Cebollera Natural Park.
region_intro: Road cycling around Almarza features routes that navigate through varied terrain, characterized by significant elevation changes and mountain passes. The region offers a network of roads that traverse rolling hills and connect rural villages. Cyclists can expect routes that climb to elevated points, providing challenging ascents and descents. The landscape is primarily defined by its mountainous character and open, rural environments. Best road cycling routes around Almarza The most popular road cycling route is Piqueras and Santa Inés Pass Loop, a 70.7 miles (113.9 km) trail that takes 5 hours 42 minutes to complete. This route features significant climbs over two mountain passes. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Magaña, Castilfrío, a difficult 52.5 miles (84.5 km) path. This route explores the rural landscape connecting the villages of Magaña and Castilfrío. Local road cyclists also love the Aldealseñor – Church of Santo Domingo loop from Arévalo de la Sierra, a 45.9 miles (73.9 km) trail leading through rural roads and past historical sites, often completed in about 3 hours 25 minutes. Road cycling around Almarza is defined by mountain passes, rolling hills, and rural roads. The network offers options for moderate to difficult ability levels. The routes in Almarza are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from a few reviews. More than 200 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Almarza's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Almarza?
A: There are over 15 road cycling routes around Almarza documented on komoot. These routes primarily navigate through varied terrain, featuring significant elevation changes and mountain passes.

Q: Are there any easy or beginner-friendly road cycling routes in Almarza?
A: The road cycling routes around Almarza are generally categorized as moderate to difficult, with no routes specifically marked as easy. Cyclists should be prepared for rolling hills and mountain passes, which define the region's terrain.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes in Almarza?
A: Road cycling in Almarza is characterized by routes that navigate through varied terrain, including significant elevation changes and mountain passes. You'll find a network of roads traversing rolling hills and connecting rural villages, offering challenging ascents and descents.

Q: Are there challenging road cycling routes for experienced cyclists?
A: Yes, Almarza offers several challenging routes. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1071173668/piqueras-and-santa-ines-pass-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Piqueras and Santa Inés Pass Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 70.7-mile (113.9 km) trail featuring significant climbs over two mountain passes. Another difficult option is &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1079299121/magana-castilfrio" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Magaña, Castilfrío&lt;/a&gt;, which covers 52.5 miles (84.5 km) through rural landscapes.

Q: What are some scenic road cycling routes with viewpoints in Almarza?
A: Many routes in Almarza offer scenic views due to the region's mountainous character. While specific viewpoints are not always marked on every route, the climbs over mountain passes like Puerto de Piqueras often provide expansive vistas. You can also find dedicated viewpoints such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3943793" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alto de Las Cabezas Geodetic Point&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3956856" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View from El Riscal Geodetic Vertex&lt;/a&gt; in the surrounding peaks.

Q: Are there circular road cycling routes available in Almarza?
A: Yes, many of the road cycling routes around Almarza are circular. An example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38248801" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Aldealseñor – Church of Santo Domingo loop from Arévalo de la Sierra&lt;/a&gt;, a 45.9-mile (73.9 km) trail, or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38248059" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Royo loop from Aeródromo de Garray&lt;/a&gt;, which is 36.9 miles (59.4 km).

Q: What can I see along the road cycling routes in Almarza?
A: Beyond the natural landscapes of rolling hills and mountain passes, you can encounter various points of interest. These include historical sites like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4000052" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cañada Honda Rock Art Site&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5587322" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castro Alto de la Cruz&lt;/a&gt;. Natural features such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3936758" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Stone Bridge over the Tera River&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3992864" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cueva de la Gallina Spring&lt;/a&gt; are also accessible.

Q: What do other road cyclists say about the routes in Almarza?
A: The road cycling routes in Almarza are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 5.0 stars from a few reviews. Over 200 road cyclists have used komoot to explore the varied terrain, often praising the challenging ascents and the open, rural environments.

Q: Are there any road cycling routes that pass through villages or offer opportunities for refreshments?
A: Many routes in Almarza connect rural villages, providing opportunities to stop for refreshments. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1079299121/magana-castilfrio" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Magaña, Castilfrío&lt;/a&gt; route explores the rural landscape connecting these two villages, where you might find local establishments.

Q: What is the typical duration for road cycling routes in Almarza?
A: Route durations vary depending on distance and difficulty. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1071173668/piqueras-and-santa-ines-pass-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Piqueras and Santa Inés Pass Loop&lt;/a&gt; typically takes around 5 hours 42 minutes, while the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20150028" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Royo – Vinuesa loop from Valdeavellano de Tera&lt;/a&gt; can be completed in about 4 hours 30 minutes.

Q: What is the best season for road cycling in Almarza?
A: Given the region's mountainous character and potential for snow on higher passes, spring, summer, and early autumn are generally the most favorable seasons for road cycling in Almarza. These periods offer more stable weather conditions and clear roads for tackling the ascents and descents.
tips: Small town located to the south of the Sierra Cebollera, to the east of the Cuerda del Pozo Reservoir. Beautiful town with its church as the center of the town.

Santa Inés is to the north of the province and it begins on its southern slope in the town of Vinuesa. Port of entity and that lasts several kilometers, where in the middle there is a detour to go to the Laguna Negra.

Villoslada de Cameros is a nucleus that is crossed by the Río Mayor, and where it is at the southern end of the province of La Rioja. It is located at the foot of the Sierra de Cebollera Natural Park.
It is a kind of balcony near the pto. Piqueras, in which you can see a large part of the Riojan mountain. It is worth making a stop to recreate yourself with a wonderful landscape.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this road cycling route?
faqs.answer: This challenging circular route is designed for race bikes and typically takes around 5 hours and 42 minutes to complete. This duration is an estimate and can vary based on your fitness level, speed, and how often you stop to enjoy the views.

faqs.question: Is this road cycling route suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: No, this route is classified as difficult. It features significant climbs, including the demanding ascents of Santa Inés Pass (1,753 m) and Piqueras Viewpoint (1,710 m). It requires very good fitness and advanced riding skills.

faqs.question: Where are the main starting points or parking areas for this route?
faqs.answer: While the route is circular, common starting points could be near settlements like Villoslada de Cameros or El Royo, which offer amenities and potential parking. The Piqueras Viewpoint itself also offers parking facilities.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on this road cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route primarily follows well-maintained roads through the Sierra de Cebollera. You'll encounter smooth, rounded mountain roads with substantial climbs and descents. The road surface is generally in very good condition, though some sections may require advanced riding skills due to the gradients.

faqs.question: What are the best times of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The best time to cycle this route is generally from late spring to early autumn. During autumn, the landscape transforms into a stunning mosaic of warm colors, making it particularly scenic. Winter cycling can be challenging due to snow and ice on the high mountain passes.

faqs.question: What natural features and wildlife might I encounter?
faqs.answer: You'll cycle through dense forests of Scots pine, beech, and oak, interspersed with high-altitude clearings and river valleys like the Iregua. The Sierra Cebollera Natural Park is rich in biodiversity, with mountain streams, waterfalls, and riparian forests. Keep an eye out for deer, roe deer, wild boar, and various forest raptors.

faqs.question: What are the key viewpoints and landmarks along the route?
faqs.answer: Key attractions include the magnificent panoramas from the Piqueras Viewpoint and Santa Inés Pass. The route also passes through charming settlements like El Royo and Villoslada de Cameros.

faqs.question: Does this route connect with other cycling paths or trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route intersects with several other paths. Notably, it covers sections of [CIMA LR13] Piqueras (17%), [CIMA SO09] Santa Inés * Montenegro de Cameros (10%), and [CIMA SO08] Piqueras (8%). You'll also find smaller portions of the Ruta del Duero and the Ruta romana del iregua.

faqs.question: Are dogs allowed on this road cycling route?
faqs.answer: This route primarily uses public roads, so dogs are generally allowed if kept under control and in accordance with local regulations. However, always ensure your dog is safe around traffic and consider their endurance for such a long and challenging ride.

faqs.question: Do I need any special permits or pay an entrance fee for the Sierra Cebollera Natural Park?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to cycle this route through the Parque Natural de Sierra Cebollera. However, please respect the natural environment and any local regulations regarding protected areas.

faqs.question: Are there places to find food, drinks, or accommodation near the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, settlements like Villoslada de Cameros and El Royo offer options for cafes, restaurants, and accommodation. It's advisable to plan your stops, especially on such a long route, to ensure you have sufficient supplies.

faqs.question: What should I know about traffic conditions on the route?
faqs.answer: While the roads are generally good, some sections, particularly on Puerto de Piqueras, can experience regular, though not constant, traffic including buses and lorries. The final part of the Piqueras climb is often quieter as a tunnel diverts some traffic. Always be aware of your surroundings and ride defensively.
